coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous detection of Cu2+ and amoxicillin based on the copper and nitrogen-regulated fluorescent probe
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Chao
Qi, Haiyan
Jing, Tao
Zhao, Ming
Li, Jun
Zhang, Jiayu
Zhu, Xiaochen
Ran, Maoxia
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence-based probe represented as Cu, N-CDs, was developed for the dual sensingdetection of Cu2+ and amoxicillin (AMO), which had a high quantum yield up to 60.08 %, along with excellent photoluminescent properties and remarkable stability. When detecting Cu2+, the fluorescence signal from Cu, N-CDs probe experienced quenching due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Subsequently, the introduction of AMO led to the recovery of the probe's fluorescence. This recovery was likely attributed to the superior affinity between Cu2+ and AMO, resulting in the displacement of Cu2+ from the Cu, N-CDs and blocked the PET process. Notably, the detection limits of Cu2+ and AMO were determined 18 nM and 9 nM, respectively. We further demonstrated the practical utility of the Cu, N-CDs probe through its successful application in the analysis of Cu2+ and AMO in Chinese herbal medicine and milk samples. Overall, our findings highlighted the potency of Cu, N-CDs as versatile and effective tools for the detection of Cu2+ and AMO, paving the way for their broad applications in the safety analysis of food and pharmaceutical.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots
Copper
Nitrogen
Amoxicillin
Fluorometric assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Turn-Off detection of Cu2+ in solution.
Turn-On detection of AMO in solution.
Detection of Cu2+ and AMO in actual sample
Mechanism underlying Cu2+ and AMO detection by Cu, N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM images (B) Size distribution; (C) XRD spectrum;(D) Infrared spectrum.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Cu, N-CDs in the presence of Cu2+;(B) The relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and Cu2+ concentrations.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Cu, N-CDs in the presence of AMO; (B) Relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and AMO concentrations.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime of Cu, N-CDs + Cu2+ and Cu, N-CDs; (B) UV spectra of Cu2+ and fluorescence of Cu, N-CDs. (C) UV spectra of Cu, N-CDs + Cu2+ and Cu, N-CDs. Zeta potential characterization of (D) Cu, N-CDs. (E) Cu2+ (F) Cu, N-CDs + Cu2+.
(A) UV spectra of Cu, N-CDs + Cu2+ and Cu, N-CDs + Cu2++AMO; (B) Zeta potential characterization of Cu, N-CDs + Cu2++AMO.
Synthesis pathway and Reaction Mechanisms of Cu, N-CDs.
Performance comparison of various methods for Cu2+ detection.
Performance comparison of different methods for AMO detection.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous detection of Cu2+ and amoxicillin based on the copper and nitrogen-regulated fluorescent probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar 161006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar 161006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence-based probe represented as Cu, N-CDs, was developed for the dual sensingdetection of Cu2+ and amoxicillin (AMO), which had a high quantum yield up to 60.08 %, along with excellent photoluminescent properties and remarkable stability. When detecting Cu2+, the fluorescence signal from Cu, N-CDs probe experienced quenching due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Subsequently, the introduction of AMO led to the recovery of the probe's fluorescence. This recovery was likely attributed to the superior affinity between Cu2+ and AMO, resulting in the displacement of Cu2+ from the Cu, N-CDs and blocked the PET process. Notably, the detection limits of Cu2+ and AMO were determined 18 nM and 9 nM, respectively. We further demonstrated the practical utility of the Cu, N-CDs probe through its successful application in the analysis of Cu2+ and AMO in Chinese herbal medicine and milk samples. Overall, our findings highlighted the potency of Cu, N-CDs as versatile and effective tools for the detection of Cu2+ and AMO, paving the way for their broad applications in the safety analysis of food and pharmaceutical.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Copper and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Within the realm of biological systems, a noteworthy category of enzymes includes copper-containing oxidases, such as ferroxidase, ascorbate oxidase, and superoxide dismutase. These enzymes play pivotal roles in catalyzing various biological transformations within the human body.[1]. Copper deficiency has been linked to a range of conditions, including functional anemia [2], Wilson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinsonism, and Central Nervous System Disease [3,4]. Conversely, excessive copper levels can be toxic, leading to conditions like hemolysis, neonatal jaundice [5], and intrahepatic cholestasis [6].

Amoxicillin (AMO) has garnered recognition for its efficacy in treating a broad spectrum of bacterial infections[7]. Nevertheless, the overuse of AMO poses substantial risks to human health, including allergies, toxic effects, and superinfections. Additionally, its environmental impact is concerning, as it contributes to the emergence and proliferation of drug-resistant bacteria[8,9].

In response to the vital concerns surrounding copper and amoxicillin, researchers have dedicated their efforts to devising innovative methods for their detection[10,11]. Presently, the land scape of conventional detection techniques dominantly encompasses AAS, Electrochemical analysis, HPLC and ICP-MS, among other methods[12–16]. However, these approaches tend to be intricate, expensive, or labor-intensive. Consequently, the utilization of fluorescence analysis has gained attention due to its numerous advantages over the a for mentioned methods.

Carbon dots (CDs) represent a novel class of nano-fluorescent materials characterized by their exceptional biocompatibility, stability, and hydrophilicity[17].These characteristics have prompted the widespread adoption of CDs in diverse applications, including sensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery[18–20]. To meet specific requirements, various strategies have been explored to engineer CDs with distinct properties. Particularly, heteroatom doping has emerged as a prominent approach, enabling the rectification of defects and the enhancement of photoluminescence properties. The general method of doping atoms is using heteroatom containing precursors, such as spirulina algae, red beetroot, arginine, m-phenylenediamine citric acid and histidine have been used as raw material to prepare doped carbon dots [21–25]. Metal ions, due to their unique electronic structures and multiple oxidation states [26–29], offer a promising avenue for achieving CDs with heightened physicochemical attributes, including modifiable fluorescence colors, increased fluorescence quantum yields[26],and the acquisition of photoinduced and photocatalytic capabilities[30,31].

In this study, we have successfully synthesized an innovative type of dual-sensing CDs, represented as Cu, N-CDs, co-doped with copper and nitrogen using a straightforward hydrothermal method. These Cu, N-CDs exhibited a remarkable fluorescence quantum yield of 60.08 %. Then, Cu, N-CDs were used as a dual sensor to detect Cu2+ and AMO. Furthermore, we extended the applicability of Cu, N-CDs to real-world samples of Chinese herbal medicine and milk, yielding favorable recovery rates. The schematic diagram of the synthesis and detection process (Scheme 1 ).

Citric, Urea, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, NaNO2, NaNO3, and NaClO2 were obtained from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Development Center. CuSO4, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, NaCl, SrCl2, CdSO4, NiSO4, NaF, AlCl3, HCl, NaOH, FeSO4, Fe2(SO4)3 and anhydrous ethanol were provided by Kai tong Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd(Tianjin, China). Amoxicillin, neomycin sulfate, chloramphenicol, clindamycin, erythromycin, sulfadimidine, metronidazole, artemisinin and Amino acids (Glycine, Alanine, Serine, Threonine, Cystine, Aspartic acid, Arginine, Histidine, Glutamic acid) were purchased from Aladdin Co, Ltd (Shanghai, China). A549 cell was obtained from the Chinese Academy of Sciences cell bank (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with high glucose was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Cell culture flasks were acquired from Corning Life Sciences (North Carolina, USA). The CCK-8 assay kit and PBS buffer were purchased from Lanjieke Co, Ltd (Anhui, China). All experimental reagents used wereanalyticallypure. Deionized water was used exclusively throughout the experimental procedures.

Cu, N-CDs were prepared via hydrothermal synthesis using urea as the nitrogen source, copper sulfate as the copper dopant, and citric acid as the carbon source. Citric acid, copper sulfate, and urea were weighed as 0.40 g, 0.20 g, 0.20 g in a mass ratio of 2:1:1, respectively. These solids were dissolved in 15 mL ultrapure water and subjected to 15 min of sonication to ensure a homogeneous dispersion. The resulting solution was transferred to reaction vessel and reacted at 180 °C in an electrically heated convection drying oven for a duration of 10 h. After cooled down, the CDs solution was centrifuged by 10,000 r/min for 15 min to remove impurities. The supernatant through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. To further refine the material, dialysis with a 500 Da cutoff membrane was carried out for a period of 48 h, followed by freeze-drying to obtain carbon dot powder, which was then stored at 4 °C.

The morphological and particle size analysis of Cu, N-CDs was conducted using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). XRD experiments were performed on Cu, N-CDs using a D8-FOCUS X-ray powder diffractometer (MA, USA). The FTIR spectrum of Cu, N-CDs was obtained using a NICOLET380 spectrometer (MA, USA). Furthermore, the element composition and surface functional groups of Cu, N-CDs were analyze using an ESCALAB250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (MA, USA). The UV–Vis spectrum experiment of Cu, N-CDs was conducted using a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). All fluorescence spectra experiments were carried out using a Shimadzu RF4301-PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed using a FLS-1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Livingston, UK). Cytotoxicity of Cu, N-CDs were was detected using an AMR-100 microplate reader (Hangzhou, China).

The optimization of Cu, N-CDs hinged on the fluorescence quantum yield, which was determined through the formula:
2
Y x
=
Y x
×
F x
F s
×
A s
A x
×
η S
η x

where Y represents the fluorescence quantum yield, F represents the integral area of the fluorescence emission peak, A represents the absorbance of the material, η represents the refractive index of the solvent in the system, and the subscript s and x represents the reference substance quinine sulfate and acquired substance, respectively[32].

Weigh an appropriate amount of freeze-dried powder (Cu, N-CDs) and final concentration was prepared. All fluorescence optical experiments under laboratory conditions at room temperature (25 °C) and tests conducted no less than three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn-Off detection of Cu2+ in solution.
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Cu2+ involved the addition of varying volumes (0–500 μL) of Cu2+ solution (10 mmol/L) to a 50 μL Cu, N-CDs solution (500 μg/mL), followed by adjusting the total volume to 2 mL with ultrapure water, thereby maintaining a Cu, N-CDs concentration of 12.5 µg/mL. The quenching effect of Cu2+ on Cu, N-CDs was evaluated by examining the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm (ex = 320), represented as (F0-F)/F0 where F0 and F respectively represent the photoluminescence intensity of Cu, N-CDs solution in the presence and absence of Cu2+. Selective experiments were conducted by substituting the Cu2+ solution with 30 μL metal ion solutions (K+, Cd2+, Ni+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Sr2+, Na+, Al3+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+), Amino acids (Glycine, Alanine, Serine, Threonine, Cystine, Aspartic acid, Arginine, Histidine, Glutamic acid) and oxidizing and reducing ions (Cl-, ClO2 -, F-, HPO4 2-, NO2 –, NO3 –) at a concentration of 10 mmol/L. Interference experiments were subsequently carried out by adding the above solutions in the presence of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn-On detection of AMO in solution.
ce:section.ce:para: To detect AMO, 200 μL of Cu2+ solution (10 mmol/L) was added to 50 μL of Cu, N-CDs solution (500 μg/mL) to quench the fluorescence. Subsequently, various volumes (0–500 μL) of AMO solution (10 mmol/L) were added to restore the fluorescence, and the total volume was adjusted to 2 mL, thereby maintaining the concentration of Cu, N-CDs at 12.5 µg/mL. The recovery effect of AMO on Cu, N-CDs was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm represented as (F0 − F)/F0, where F0 and F indicated the photoluminescence intensity of Cu, N-CDs solution in the presence and absence of AMO, respectively. Selective experiments were conducted by replacing the AMO solution with 30 μL of various drug molecule solution (Kanamycin, Sulfamethazine, Erythromycin, Florfenicol, Clarithromycin, Metronidazole, Chlortetracycline hydrochloride, and Artemisinin), Amino acids (Glycine, Alanine, Serine, Threonine, Cystine, Aspartic acid, Arginine, Histidine, Glutamic acid), and oxidizing and reducing ions (Cl-, ClO2 -, F-, HPO4 2-, NO2 –, NO3 –). each at a concentration of 10 mmol/L. Subsequently, interference resistance experiments were conducted by adding these solution in the presence of AMO.

In this experience, the Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) sample with pretreatment were placed at room temperature and shaken well. We added 50 µL Cu, N-CDs and 100 µL TCM sample to the fluorescent cell, then diluted with deionized water to 2000 µL for detecting the initial content of Cu2+.Different amounts of Cu2+ (2, 4, 6 µmol/L) were added into the mixed solution and the recovery rates were calculated. Preprocessing methods are inspired by the literature-Tu et al., 2021).

200 μL of Cu2+ solution (10 mmol/L), 50 µL Cu, N-CDs and 100 μL treated milk sample were added to fluorescent cell, then diluted with deionized water to 2000 µL for detecting the initial content of AMO. Different amounts of AMO (5, 15, and 30 μmol/L, 10, 20, and 30 μmol/L) were added into the mixed solution and recovery rates were calculated. Preprocessing methods are inspired by the literature[33].

The cytotoxicity of Cu, N-CDs was evaluated using the MTT assay. A549 cells were seeded a 96-well plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells/mL. A series of different concentrations of Cu, N-CDs solutions (200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 μg/mL) were added to different wells and further incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the cell viability was measured by CCK-8. 20 μ L of CCK-8 were added to each well, and the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. The wavelength at 480 nm was measured, and the assay was repeated at least three times.
Cell viability (%）= (OD experimental group / OD control group) × 100 %.

To identify the optimal synthesis conditions for CDs with the evaluation criterion being fluorescence quantum yield, we systematically examined nine different synthesis conditions by manipulating key experimental variables, including synthesis temperature, synthesis time, and the mass ratio of citric acid, urea, and copper sulfate pentahydrate (Table S1). The fluorescence quantum yields for these nine CDs were summarized in the table. It was evident that under the synthesis conditions of 180 °C and a mass ratio of citric acid, urea, and copper sulfate pentahydrate of 2:1:1, a reaction time of 10 h, a carbon dot labeled as CDS4 exhibited the highest fluorescence quantum yield, with a remarkable value of 60.08 %. Consequently, CDS4 was selected for further investigation in subsequent experiments and was denominated as Cu, N-CDs.

The HRTEM imaging provided insights into the microstructure and morphology of Cu, N-CDs, which displayed spherical structures with excellent dispersibility (Fig. 2
A). Moreover, the Cu, N-CDs revealed distinct crystal lattices with a spacing of 0.2 nm, aligning with the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon[34]. Further insights of Cu, N-CDs revealed a significant XRD diffraction peak around 2θ = 24° (Fig. 2C), reflecting good crystallinity (Fig. 2A inset), thus confirming the graphite-like structure of Cu, N-CDs. The radius of Cu, N-CDs spanned from 1.0 to 4.5 nm, with an average diameter of 2.5 nm (Fig. 2B). The infrared spectrum of Cu, N-CDs (Fig. 2D) characterized the surface structure and functional groups, revealing distinctive features. The absorption peak appeared 3270 cm−1 ∼ 3600 cm−1 due to the unsaturated stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H bond[35]. Absorption peaks at 2920 and 2860 cm−1 were attributed to saturated stretching vibrations of C-H bonds, while peaks at 1710 and 1690 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibrations of C = O bonds[36]. Furthermore, absorption bands observed at 1400 and 1170 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibrations of C-N bonds. This comprehensive infrared spectrum confirmed the presence of functional groups, including –COOH, –NH2, –OH, signifying robust water solubility of Cu, N-CDs[37,38].

The elemental composition and surface functional groups of Cu, N-CDs were further investigated using XPS (Fig. S1). The full-range XPS spectrum of Cu, N-CDs displayed four distinct peaks at 284.8, 400.08, 831.12, and 935.2 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Cu2p, respectively (Fig. S1A). These peaks were used to calculate the atomic contents, which were measured at 59.92 %, 6.00 %, 33.16 %, and 0.92 %, respectively (Fig. S1B). Further in-depth analysis of the four elements was performed via High-resolution XPS spectra. The high-resolution C1s spectrum revealed three characteristic peaks at 284.08, 285.38, and 287.88 eV, corresponding to C-C, C = O/C-N, and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. S1C) [36]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum displayed two characteristic peaks at 399.18 and 400.78 eV, representing C-N and N-H bonds, respectively (Fig. S1D)[39]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum exhibited two characteristic peaks at 530.98 and 532.38 eV, corresponding to C = O and O-H/C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. S1E) [35]. The high-resolution Cu2p (Fig. S1F) spectrum included three characteristic peaks with two peaks at 934.76 and 952.60 eV representing the spin–orbit splitting of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 and the peak at 932.79 eV corresponding to Cu+ [40]. These results were consistent with the infrared spectroscopy results.

Next, we examined the optical properties of Cu, N-CDs to reveal their comprehensive characteristics. The UV–vis absorption spectrum displayed a prominent absorption peak at 330 nm, attributable to the n → π* transition of valence electrons (Fig. S2A). This observation indicated the presence of C = O groups and conjugated structures within Cu, N-CDs, aligning with the results from the infrared spectrum. The optimal excitation wavelength of Cu, N-CDs was 320 nm, with an emission wavelength of 420 nm (Fig. S2A). Cu, N-CDs solutions appeared colorless but exhibited intense blue fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV, 365 nm) illumination (insets in Fig. S2A), which was consistent with the CIE color coordinates (0.1463, 0.0583) (Fig. S2B). Cu, N-CDs demonstrated excitation-dependent emission, with the emission peak shifting by approximately 30 nm as the excitation wavelength ranged from 300 to 380 nm (Fig. S2C, S2D).

The photostability of Cu, N-CDs under UV illumination was evaluated (Fig. S3A). Following exposure to continuous UV irradiation for 90 min, the intensity of Cu, N-CDs did not exhibit a significant decline, indicating excellent photostability. In addition, the influence of NaCl (ranging from 0 to 1.0 M) on the fluorescence properties of Cu, N-CDs was assessed. The intensity remained consistent (Fig. S3B), demonstrating the remarkable salt resistance of Cu, N-CDs. This property enhances the potential of our Cu, N-CDs for various sensing applications under more complex and physiological conditions. The impact of pH (pH 1.0 to 14.0) on Cu, N-CDs was investigated (Fig. S3C). The intensity of Cu, N-CDs were significantly affected at pH 1, with intensity gradually increasing within the pH range of 2 to 9, and then gradually decreasing within the pH range of 10 to 14. Notably, Cu, N-CDs displayed slow decay and maintained relatively stable intensity across the entire pH range of 1 to 14, highlighting their adaptability to a broad spectrum of acidic and alkaline environments.

The intensity of Cu, N-CDs gradually decreased upon the introduction of varying concentrations of Cu2+ (Fig. 3A). Remarkably, when the concentration of Cu2+ reached 100 μmol/L, quenched to 43.61 %. Fig 6B further revealed a linear relationship between the concentration of Cu2+ within the ranges of 0.06–10 μmol/L, 10–35 μmol/L, and 35–60 μmol/L, all exhibiting proportional fluorescence quenching efficiency. The corresponding linear equations were calculated as (F0-F)/F0 = 0.01663[Cu2+]-0.0047 (R2 = 0.997), (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0058[Cu2+] + 0.1041 (R2 = 0.996), and (F0-F)/F0 = 0.00237[Cu2+] + 0.2083 (R2 = 0.993), respectively. Importantly, combining the linear relationship, the detection limit was calculated to be 0.0183 μmol/L (3δ/k). Notably, our Cu, N-CDs exhibited a lower detection limit and a broader detection range when compared to other methods for Cu2+ detection (Table 1 ).

In order to demonstrate the specificity of Cu, N-CDs towards Cu2+, potentially interfering ions, including various metal ions (K+, Cd2+, Ni+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Sr2+, Na+, Al3+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+), Amino acids (Glycine, Alanine, Serine, Threonine, Cystine, Aspartic acid, Arginine, Histidine, Glutamic acid) and oxidizing and reducing ions (Cl-, ClO2 -, F-, HPO4 2-, NO2 –, NO3 –) were examined. It was observed that only Cu2+ significantly quenched the emission intensity (Fig. S4A). Furthermore, when these potential interference ions coexisted with Cu2+, their presence did not affect the fluorescence quenching induced by Cu2+ (Fig. S4B). Collectively, these results suggested the high selectivity of Cu, N-CDs for detecting Cu2+.

To examine the selectivity of the Cu, N-CDs + Cu2+ system, nine drugs (Kanamycin, Sulfamethazine, Erythromycin, Florfenicol, Clarithromycin, Metronidazole, Chlortetracycline hydrochloride, and Artemisinin), Amino acids (Glycine, Alanine, Serine, Threonine, Cystine, Aspartic acid, Arginine, Histidine, Glutamic acid) and oxidizing and reducing ions (Cl-, ClO2 -, F-, HPO4 2-, NO2 –, NO3 –) were investigated. The system exhibited the highest response only to AMO when each of the drugs was added (Fig. S5A). Importantly, when these above-mentioned interference substances were introduced with the presence of AMO, were introduced with the presence of AMO, their coexistence had negligible effect, highlighting the robust anti-interference capability of the Cu, N-CDs + Cu2+ system for detecting AMO (Fig. S5B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and AMO in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical applicability of Cu, N-CDs, were analyzed the contents of Cu2+ and AMO in actual samples. The concentration of Cu2+ in three distinct Chinese herbal medicines (Ardisia crenata, Glabrous Greenbrier Rhizome, and Clove) was analyzed (Table S2). Notably, the Cu2+ contents in Glabrous Greenbrier Rhizome and Clove were measured at 0.778 μmol/L and 0.880 μmol/L, whereas no Cu2+ were detected in Ardisia crenata. Additionally, three different concentrations of Cu2+(2, 4, 6 μmol/L) were added to Chinese herbal medicine samples to check the effectivenessofthismethod. As expected, the recovery rate was observed to be in the range of 89.09 % to 115.27 % (RSD < 6.45 %) (Table S2). Furthermore, the capability of the Cu, N-CDs + Cu2+ system as a fluorescent sensor for AMO detection was evaluated in milk samples. Cartons of milk (Sample 1) and bulk milk (Sample 2) were acquired from a local supermarket and a farmer’s market, respectively. The analysis revealed an AMO content of 7.583 μmol/Lin Sample 2, while no AMO was detected in Sample 1. Additionally, AMO solutions at concentrations of 5, 15, and 30 μmol/L, as well as 10, 20, and 30 μmol/L, were added to Sample 1 and Sample 2, respectively. The recovery rate was found to be within the range of 95.46 % to 116.6 %(RSD < 4.52 %) (Table S3). Overall, these results underscored the high accuracy of our Cu, N-CDs and their significant potential for practical application in the detection of Cu2+ and AMO contents in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism underlying Cu2+ and AMO detection by Cu, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the underlying mechanism driving the detection of Cu2+ by Cu, N-CDs, we explored the fluorescence lifetimes of Cu, N-CDs both in nonexistence theandexistence of Cu2+. The average fluorescence lifetime of Cu, N-CDs was measured to be 10.6849 ns (T0) and decreased to 9.8005 ns (T)upon the introduction of Cu2+.The observation of T0 not equating to T suggested that Cu, N-CDs underwent collision events with Cu2+, indicating of a dynamic quenching process (Fig. 5 A). As shown Fig. 5B, there was no overlap between the absorption spectrum of Cu2+ and fluorescence spectrum of Cu, N-CDs, which indicated that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer could not occur. Additionally, after the introduction of Cu2+, no markedly alteration was observed (Fig. 5C), indicating the possibility of excited-state molecules of Cu, N-CDs collided with Cu2+ to form an exciplex and a charge transfer mechanism between Cu, N-CDs and Cu2+. Given the abundance of functional group (–OH, C = O) on the surface of Cu, N-CDs, zeta potential was observed to be −35.7 mV (Fig. 5D). However, with the addition of Cu2+ (15.3 mV) (Fig. 5E), the zeta potential shifted to −10.2 mV (Fig. 5F), further implying the occurrence of a charge transfer process between Cu, N-CDs and Cu2+. Overall, we suspected that the occurrence of a PET process between the Cu, N-CDs and Cu2+.

Conversely, when AMO was introduced into the Cu, N-CDs + Cu2+ complex system, we observed a notable change in the absorption peak (Fig. 6A). A new absorption peak emerged at 250 nm, and the peak at 350 nm exhibited an approximately 8 nm blue shift. These results might be attributed to the formation of novel substances from the interaction between Cu2+ and AMO. Zeta potential results demonstrated a potential change to −11.2 mV following the introduction of AMO (Fig. 6B), signifying a charge transfer process within the system. We postulated that Cu2+, driven by a higher affinity for AMO compared to Cu, N-CDs, was displaced from the surface of Cu, N-CDs + Cu2+ complex system. Consequently, the Cu2+ initially adsorbed on the surface of the system were reduced, blocking of the PET process, leading to fluorescence recovery [49].

Finally, the cytotoxicity of Cu, N-CDs in A549 cells with MTT assay was carried out. Even when A549 cells were exposed to 1000 μ g/mL of Cu, N-CDs, the cell viability still showed above 80.72 %(Fig.6S). The result indicated the low cytotoxicity of our Cu, N-CDs.

In summary, we have synthesized novel dual-sensing CDs doped with copper and nitrogen, denoted as Cu, N-CDs, via a one-step hydrothermal method. The Cu, N-CDs exhibit exceptional selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Cu2+ and AMO, with detection limits as low as 18 nM and 9 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the practical applicability of Cu, N-CDs was demonstrated through the successful detection of Cu2+ and AMO in real samples of Chinese medicine and milk, respectively. Additionally, Cu, N-CDs showed low cytotoxicity. In conclusion, our Cu, N-CDs hold great promise for future applications in the detection of Cu2+ and AMO in the fields of food and pharmaceutical sample detection.

Chao Luo: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Haiyan Qi: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tao Jing: Investigation, Data curation. Ming Zhao: Data curation. Jun Li: Funding acquisition. Jiayu Zhang: Resources. Xiaochen Zhu: Investigation, Conceptualization. Maoxia Ran: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172092); the Special Scientific Research Fund of Powerful Province Through Higher Education (GJQSCYH-2022001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110471.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ascorbic acid oxidase-based sensing platform for stereoselective Interaction with ascorbic acid and isoascorbic acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ma, J.J. Xu, C.C. Yang, J.Y. Song, Y.Z. Fu, An Ascorbic Acid Oxidase-based Sensing Platform for Stereoselective Interaction with Ascorbic Acid and Isoascorbic Acid, ANALYTICAL SCIENCES, 34(2018) 427-432.
